what_it_does: A design-system specimen page that shows the four theme modes side by side — Light (default, no attribute), Dim (data-theme="dim"), Dark (data-theme="dark"), and Contrast (data-theme="contrast") — by rendering the same small UI fragment (zone swatch row, a candidate card with chips and Back/Approve buttons) in each themed pane. Demonstrates that one ground knob (data-theme) flips the whole zone system while gold discipline is preserved. Links ../styles.css and builds each pane from a JS PANES array.
observations: @dsCard group="Colors" header; subtitle: 'One ground knob flips the whole zone system — gold discipline preserved'.
Four .pane elements each set their own data-theme attribute (empty for light, then dim/dark/contrast).

Each pane renders the same content: a row of zone swatches (content/source/success/warning/reject + the vi/gold zone) and a card with a 'ready' chip, a 'Vi pick' gold chip, and secondary/primary buttons.
Primary button uses --accent-gold with a hardcoded #1f1a12 text colour; the page body background is a hardcoded #cfc7b6.
Zone swatches and card chrome resolve from --zone-<k>-surface/-edge and --vi-surface/--vi-edge tokens so they recompute per theme via the inherited data-theme.
Card copy explicitly states: 'The same zone tokens, recomputed against this theme's ground. Gold stays the only saturated voice.'
Uses --elev-4/--elev-1 for pane and card elevation and the rotated-gold-gem motif in each pane heading.
Responsive: grid collapses to one column under 720px.
standouts: Demonstrates the theme system as a single inherited knob (data-theme on a container) flipping every token-derived surface — the same markup recomputed four ways.
Reinforces the gold-discipline rule across all four grounds (gold stays the only saturated voice in light/dim/dark/contrast).
conventions: Follows the specimen pattern: @dsCard header + self-contained HTML linking the shared stylesheet, panes built by inline JS.
Themes are applied via the data-theme attribute knob (matches the axis-dial convention described in the README/CLAUDE docs).
Most colour resolves via --zone-*/--vi-* tokens, but the body background (#cfc7b6) and primary-button text (#1f1a12) are hardcoded literals in the local style.
Uses the rotated-gold-gem heading motif consistent with sibling specimens.
concerns: Two hardcoded hex literals in the local <style> (#cfc7b6 body bg, #1f1a12 primary-button text) sit outside the token system; the ink literal in particular duplicates what an --on-gold/ink token presumably already defines.
notes: The specimen proving the theme axis: one data-theme knob recomputes the same zone-token UI across Light/Dim/Dark/Contrast while gold stays reserved — read it to see how theming is a single inherited dial.
questions: Should the primary-button text (#1f1a12) use the --on-gold token mentioned in the README instead of a hardcoded ink value, and is #cfc7b6 page background intentional rather than a token?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the subtitle promises one ground knob flipping the zone system with gold discipline preserved, and the four themed panes demonstrate exactly that.
apparent_themes: Theme as a single orthogonal axis (data-theme knob)
intention_signals: Trying to prove that theming is one inherited knob over a token system — the same UI fragment recomputes correctly across four grounds without per-theme markup — and that the gold rule holds in every mode.
novelty: Showing all four themes as live, simultaneously-rendered panes of identical markup makes the 'one knob flips everything' claim directly verifiable side by side.
contribution: Contributes a side-by-side demonstration of the theme axis (light/dim/dark/contrast) operating as a single data-theme knob over the zone tokens, with the gold-discipline invariant shown holding across all four grounds.
summary_for_embedding: A ConceptV design-system specimen HTML page rendering the same zone-token UI fragment across four themes (light/dim/dark/contrast) via the data-theme knob, showing themes recompute from tokens while gold stays the only saturated voice.
intention_for_embedding: To prove theming is a single inherited axis-knob that recomputes the whole zone system across four grounds while preserving the gold-reserved-for-decisions rule.
